name: Indie Hacker / Startup Stack
description: Near-zero infra overhead; AI handles 60-80% of boilerplate. The fastest path from idea to deployed SaaS.
target: Solo founders and small teams shipping full-stack web apps fast
mission: You have 2 weeks to validate an idea. You're the only engineer. Your runway is 3 months.

not_in_stack.reason: Orchestration overhead kills solo velocity at this stage
GPU ops are a full-time job you don't have
Don't add a Postgres instance just to prove a concept

kill_conditions: Team grows beyond 3 engineers
Any compliance or data residency requirement appears
Request volume exceeds 50k/day consistently
why: Solo founders need to ship fast. This stack maximises AI leverage at every layer — v0 generates the UI, Cursor edits the code, Claude Code handles multi-step terminal tasks, the Vercel AI SDK manages streaming LLM calls, and Langfuse keeps traces so you know when something breaks. Practically zero ops overhead.
tradeoffs: Tight coupling to the Vercel/OpenAI ecosystem. Works best for standard web apps — not the right fit for high-throughput workloads, regulated data, or teams with strict compliance requirements.

name: AI Design-to-Code Pipeline
description: Eliminates the design-to-code translation cost. Describe a component in plain English, get production-quality React and Tailwind back — then refine with AI in Cursor.
target: Design–engineering handoff, component libraries, and rapid prototyping
mission: Your designer keeps handing off Figma files that take 3 days to implement. You need to turn a prompt or a screenshot into a production component in under an hour.

not_in_stack.reason: No pipeline orchestration needed — it's a linear design-to-component flow
No LLM calls to observe; the AI generation happens inside the design tools
Direct API access adds complexity — v0 and Locofy already wrap it
Locofy is purpose-built for Figma-to-code. Use it in place of v0 if your starting point is a Figma file — not a text prompt.

kill_conditions: Design system grows complex enough to need API spec governance
Component count exceeds what AI can reliably generate without drift
Team needs reproducible outputs with spec-level guarantees
why: v0 turns a prompt into production React/Tailwind — the starting point is already deployable, not a wireframe. From there, Cursor refines it inline with full codebase context so edits stay coherent across files. Claude Code handles the cross-file ripple effects: when a component changes, it tracks down every import, test, and usage without you mapping the blast radius. Qodo generates test coverage automatically for the AI-produced code — so nothing ships without a safety net. The critical handoff is v0 → Cursor → Claude Code: each step takes the output of the previous one and makes it production-grade.
tradeoffs: AI-generated code often needs cleanup, especially for non-standard designs. Works best for established design systems and common component patterns. Final output quality is tightly coupled to the quality and consistency of your design inputs.

name: MCP Power User Stack
description: MCP turns your AI into an agent that can browse, write to repos, and execute code — one protocol, full reach.
target: Individual power users and small teams wanting unified AI orchestration across all their tools
mission: You're running 4 different AI tools with no shared context. You want one AI that can touch your repo, browse the web, and run code without copy-pasting between windows.

not_in_stack.reason: Full orchestration framework is overkill when MCP handles tool dispatch natively
You're not building a product — you're augmenting your own workflow
Personal-use workflows don't yet need production observability overhead

kill_conditions: Security audit flags broad MCP server access permissions
Team size grows and shared access governance is needed
Custom server count exceeds what manual maintenance allows
why: MCP eliminates the need for separate AI integrations per tool. Cursor and Claude Code connect directly to MCP servers — GitHub MCP for live repo access, Playwright MCP for browser automation, E2B for safe code execution. Smithery is your registry for discovering pre-built community servers. The MCP TypeScript SDK is what you reach for when you need to build a custom server that none of those cover.
tradeoffs: The MCP ecosystem is still maturing — some servers are experimental and break. The security model requires careful server vetting; each server gets broad access by design. Not suitable for sensitive environments without strict server auditing.

name: No-Code AI Automation Stack
description: Full AI workflow automation without writing code. n8n handles event triggers and system connections; Flowise builds the LLM reasoning chains that run inside those workflows.
target: Non-technical teams, operators, and PMs who need AI workflows without an engineering bottleneck
mission: Your ops team needs to automate lead enrichment, document summarization, and Slack alerts using AI — and the engineering queue is 6 weeks long.

not_in_stack.reason: Requires Python and graph programming knowledge — defeats the point
Code-first SDK; the target user doesn't write TypeScript
IDE with AI assistance still requires writing code
Dify competes directly with Flowise for the visual LLM-builder role — pick one platform, not both.

kill_conditions: Workflow complexity exceeds what visual builders can express without workarounds
Team needs version control and CI/CD on workflow logic
Performance requirements demand custom-optimized pipelines
why: n8n owns the 'when and what' — it watches for events (new lead, form submission, scheduled job) and decides what data flows downstream. Flowise owns the 'how the AI reasons' — it takes that payload, runs it through a visual LLM chain, and returns a structured result. n8n then routes the result onward: Slack alert, CRM update, email reply. The two tools have opposite design philosophies — n8n is about connections between systems, Flowise is about reasoning inside an LLM call — and together they cover the full automation loop. Langfuse captures every model call Flowise makes, so you can see where quality degrades before your ops team notices.
tradeoffs: Visual builders hit a ceiling fast. Complex branching logic, custom data transforms, and stateful multi-step agents require code eventually. Vendor lock-in to hosted no-code platforms can become a migration cost as complexity grows.

name: TypeScript-Only AI Stack
description: Full AI product capability — agents, RAG, observability — without a single line of Python.
target: Frontend and full-stack teams who live in TypeScript and refuse to introduce a Python service
mission: Your team ships TypeScript. Someone suggested LangChain. You've decided you'd rather not introduce a Python microservice just to call an LLM.

not_in_stack.reason: Primary SDK is Python-first; TypeScript port lags significantly behind
Python-native orchestration — Mastra covers the same ground in TypeScript
TypeScript version is a second-class citizen compared to the Python library

kill_conditions: Need to use ML libraries that only exist in Python (PyTorch, HuggingFace training)
Team hires ML engineers who expect a Python stack
Fine-tuning or custom model training becomes a requirement
why: The Vercel AI SDK makes every LLM call type-safe — streaming responses, tool definitions, and structured outputs all carry TypeScript types so integration bugs surface at compile time, not in production. Mastra builds agent workflows on top of that foundation with typed memory primitives, so agent state is part of your type system rather than an opaque blob. Qdrant's TypeScript client means retrieval results arrive with known shapes too. Langfuse completes the chain with typed trace events. The point isn't developer preference: it's that type safety propagates through the entire AI call stack, giving you the same correctness guarantees on AI code that you have on the rest of your backend.
tradeoffs: TypeScript AI tooling is 6–12 months behind its Python equivalents in feature depth. Complex RAG patterns and fine-tuning pipelines will eventually force a Python boundary. Some research-grade tooling has no TypeScript port at all.

name: Zero-Budget OSS Stack
description: Build a fully capable AI product with $0/mo in API costs. Everything runs locally; nothing phones home.
target: Bootstrapped projects, OSS contributors, and developers who want capability without recurring API bills
mission: You have a working laptop, a GitHub account, and zero budget for API subscriptions. You want a capable AI coding and Q&A setup anyway.

not_in_stack.reason: Every token costs money — that's the constraint you're solving around
The cloud-hosted tier defeats the zero-egress goal; self-host it if you need tracing
Commercial product with a subscription; Continue gives 80% of the value for free

kill_conditions: Project goes to production and output quality ceiling of local models becomes a problem
Inference speed requirements exceed what consumer hardware can deliver
Team size grows and centralized model serving is needed
why: Ollama is the inference server that makes everything else free — it runs Llama, Mistral, and Qwen locally and exposes an endpoint that looks exactly like OpenAI's API. Aider, Continue, and LlamaIndex all talk to that same endpoint: Aider for autonomous coding tasks, Continue for inline IDE suggestions, LlamaIndex for RAG pipelines. Chroma stores embeddings in-process with no network call. The architecture is deliberately drop-in: every tool uses the same OpenAI-compatible interface, so if you outgrow local inference you swap the endpoint URL and nothing else changes. The total operational cost is electricity.
tradeoffs: Local models top out at 70B parameters on consumer hardware. Output quality is noticeably below GPT-4o or Claude for complex reasoning tasks. Setup friction is higher than calling an API. Latency depends entirely on your machine.

name: Agentic Coding Stack
description: Full loop from task to reviewed PR without human keyboard input. AI reads, writes, executes, and ships.
target: Teams delegating feature development, bug fixing, and refactoring to AI agents
mission: Your backlog has 60 tickets. Half of them are well-specified, repetitive, and boring. You want an agent to close them while your engineers work on the hard ones.

not_in_stack.reason: SDK for building AI products, not for running autonomous coding agents
Visual builder for workflows, not for executing code against a live repo
Replaced by LangSmith here — tracing needs tighter integration with the LangGraph-based agent loop

kill_conditions: Task specs are too ambiguous for autonomous execution without constant human correction
Security policy prohibits autonomous agents with write access to production repos
Agent failure rate on complex tasks exceeds cost of human engineering time
why: Close the loop between AI writing code and it being reviewed, tested, and merged. Claude Code reads the repo via GitHub MCP, executes safely in E2B sandboxes, and gets reviewed by CodeRabbit before any human sees the PR. OpenHands handles longer autonomous runs — it also executes in E2B and submits PRs that go through the same review gate.
tradeoffs: Autonomous agents diverge without clear task specs and tight scope. Requires disciplined prompt engineering and explicit task boundaries. Debugging agent failures mid-run is non-trivial.

name: Multi-Agent DevOps Stack
description: Specialised agents working in parallel — one triages, one fixes, one reviews. Dramatically reduces cycle time.
target: Engineering teams automating issue triage, code fixes, and PR review
mission: Your on-call rotation spends 40% of time on toil — triaging issues, writing boilerplate fixes, reviewing PRs that follow obvious patterns. You want agents to own that loop entirely.

not_in_stack.reason: Visual workflow builder — lacks the programmatic control needed for multi-agent coordination
App-layer platform, not suitable for CI/CD pipeline orchestration
Direct API calls add fragility — LangGraph manages model routing and retry logic

kill_conditions: Agent coordination complexity grows beyond what LangGraph state machines can express cleanly
Security team requires human-in-the-loop approval before any automated merge
Multi-agent token costs exceed the engineering time saved
why: Specialised agents working in parallel reduce cycle time dramatically. LangGraph coordinates the handoffs — it assigns tasks to Claude Code for implementation and Jules for async VM-based execution, keeps agents wired to the live repo state via GitHub MCP, and routes all PRs to CodeRabbit for review before any human sees them. Jules runs tasks in isolated VMs overnight and returns PRs; Claude Code handles interactive or complex tasks that need reasoning mid-run. LangSmith traces the full multi-agent execution graph so you can diagnose exactly where a failure occurred.
tradeoffs: Multi-agent coordination adds latency and token cost. Clear agent boundaries are required to prevent conflicts. Debugging inter-agent failures mid-run is non-trivial and requires good tracing from day one.

name: Browser AI / Web Agent Stack
description: An AI that navigates the web, fills forms, scrapes data, and monitors pages — with no human in the loop.
target: Teams automating web research, competitive monitoring, form submission, and browser-based workflows
mission: You have a workflow that requires navigating 12 different websites, filling forms, and aggregating the results into a report. A human does it every Monday. That stops now.

not_in_stack.reason: Frontend SDK — irrelevant when the browser is the tool, not the UI layer
RAG pipeline framework; the data source here is live web pages, not a document corpus
Visual builder doesn't expose the low-level browser control this stack requires

kill_conditions: Target sites implement bot detection that breaks Playwright automation
Task complexity requires judgment that exceeds current model capabilities mid-session
Legal review flags scraping of specific domains
why: Playwright MCP gives Claude Code and Cursor direct browser control — navigation, clicks, form fills, screenshots, and DOM inspection. E2B provides a sandboxed environment so browser sessions run safely without touching your local machine. LangGraph orchestrates multi-step web tasks with state and retry logic. Langfuse traces every action so you can debug when an agent gets stuck on an unexpected page state.
tradeoffs: Browser agents are brittle against UI changes — a redesigned login page breaks the flow. Anti-bot measures on major platforms can block Playwright-based automation. Real-time web state makes deterministic testing hard.

name: Voice AI Pipeline
description: The managed-first voice AI pipeline: Vapi handles telephony, VAD, and turn management; Deepgram provides streaming STT; ElevenLabs speaks; OpenAI API reasons.
target: Teams building voice assistants, call automation, real-time transcription, or audio-first AI products
mission: Your support team handles 500 calls a day. 60% are the same 10 questions. You want an AI voice agent that handles the tier-1 queue end-to-end.

not_in_stack.reason: Frontend streaming SDK — voice pipeline latency is managed by Vapi's own WebRTC layer, not a UI streaming library
General model hosting can't match the sub-300ms STT latency that Deepgram's streaming API delivers
OSS frame-based pipeline for teams who need code-level control over the STT→LLM→TTS loop — graduate to it when Vapi's abstractions become a ceiling

kill_conditions: Latency requirements fall below 300ms round-trip, requiring dedicated real-time infrastructure
Domain-specific vocabulary accuracy demands a fine-tuned speech model
Call volume requires a purpose-built telephony platform instead of a custom pipeline
why: Vapi handles everything a real voice product needs that a generic LLM stack doesn't: WebRTC session management, voice activity detection, barge-in handling, and turn orchestration. You write a system prompt and Vapi handles the rest. Deepgram provides streaming STT at ~300ms latency — roughly 3× faster than OpenAI Whisper for real-time use. ElevenLabs provides the most natural TTS available. OpenAI API handles the LLM reasoning turn. Langfuse traces every call turn so you can measure latency, detect failure modes, and curate training data from real conversations. The stack is deliberately managed-first: you get production-grade voice without owning the WebRTC infrastructure.
tradeoffs: Vapi's abstraction layer limits control over low-level pipeline behaviour — custom VAD models, multi-speaker diarisation, and custom interruption logic require migrating to Pipecat or LiveKit. Domain-specific vocabulary (medical codes, product names) may degrade Deepgram accuracy and require a fine-tuned STT model. At high call volumes the per-minute pricing on Vapi + Deepgram + ElevenLabs stacks up quickly.

name: Data + AI Pipeline
description: Your data warehouse becomes the context source. LLM layers sit on top of structured transforms, not raw text.
target: Data teams and analytics engineers adding AI reasoning to existing data pipelines
mission: You have a mature data warehouse. Your analysts want to query it in plain English, get summaries of anomalies, and trigger automated reports. The answer is not a chatbot over a CSV.

not_in_stack.reason: Frontend streaming SDK — the pipeline runs server-side on scheduled triggers, not user requests
IDE assistant doesn't integrate with scheduled pipeline orchestration
Visual builder lacks the data transformation depth this pipeline requires

kill_conditions: Data volume requires distributed processing beyond what LlamaIndex handles natively
Real-time streaming data replaces batch transforms as the primary source
Compliance requires audit trails on every data access that the current stack can't provide
why: n8n bridges the warehouse and the AI layer — it schedules syncs, handles authentication to source systems, and passes raw records downstream without custom ETL scripts. LlamaIndex takes those records and makes them retrieval-ready: chunking, embedding, and indexing them so queries can find relevant context by meaning rather than SQL. pgvector stores the embeddings inside the same Postgres instance your application already queries — no separate vector infrastructure to operate or keep synchronized. OpenAI API handles both embedding the incoming query and generating the response from retrieved context. Langfuse traces every retrieval event: which documents were fetched, what the model received, and where the answer drifted from ground truth. The leverage: your warehouse data becomes semantically queryable without leaving your existing infrastructure.
tradeoffs: Text-to-SQL is still unreliable for complex multi-join queries. LLMs hallucinate column names and table relationships without careful schema injection. This stack works best on well-documented schemas; poorly named tables produce poor answers.

name: LLM Production Infra Stack
description: Provider-agnostic from day one. Swap models without touching app code; full cost and quality visibility.
target: AI-native startups and teams shipping LLM-powered products to production
mission: You've shipped a product on OpenAI. The bill just hit $8k/mo. Anthropic released a better model for your use case at half the price. Switching should take a config change, not a sprint.

not_in_stack.reason: Abstractions add complexity when LiteLLM already handles the provider normalization layer
Stateful agent orchestration is overkill for a request-response LLM product
Full RAG framework; Qdrant's native clients handle retrieval without the extra abstraction

kill_conditions: Workflow complexity grows to require stateful multi-step agent orchestration
Team needs fine-grained access control and enterprise SSO on the observability layer
Volume hits a scale where the proxy layer latency becomes a product constraint
why: The Vercel AI SDK is the app's entry point — it handles streaming UIs and chains LLM calls through LiteLLM, which normalises every provider API so you can swap models with a config change. LiteLLM routes to OpenAI or Anthropic and pipes traces into Langfuse for per-request cost and quality visibility. Qdrant handles semantic retrieval when context needs to come from your own data.
tradeoffs: The proxy layer adds latency (~10–30ms overhead). Requires discipline to define meaningful evals early — bad evals give false confidence. Cost control is critical at volume; easy to miss runaway usage without alerting.

name: Evaluation & Quality Stack
description: "It works" is not a deploy criterion. Promptfoo catches regressions in CI before they ship; Langfuse observes every production call and curates failure datasets; Braintrust runs structured experiments to compare prompt versions and model upgrades.
target: Teams shipping LLM features that need measurable, reproducible quality guarantees
mission: You've had three incidents where a model update silently broke the product's output quality. No one noticed for a week. That doesn't happen again.

not_in_stack.reason: Agent orchestration — irrelevant when the objective is measuring output quality, not running agents
Provider routing; this stack assumes you're already settled on providers and need to evaluate them
Retrieval pipeline framework — evaluation here is model-and-prompt focused, not retrieval-focused
Promptfoo covers the CI regression testing role; DeepEval shines in Python-only stacks where it's the sole eval tool rather than one of several.

kill_conditions: Eval datasets reveal that prompting has hit its ceiling and fine-tuning is the next lever
Model quality gaps are traced to retrieval quality rather than generation quality
Team needs a dedicated ML platform with experiment versioning beyond what Braintrust provides
why: Most teams skip evals until production breaks. This stack makes eval a first-class engineering discipline: Promptfoo runs prompt regression suites in CI; DeepEval gives structured quality metrics (faithfulness, relevance, hallucination rate); Langfuse captures production traces and curates them into eval datasets; Braintrust manages experiments and dataset versioning so you can track quality over time.
tradeoffs: Evals require upfront investment in dataset curation and metric definition that most teams underestimate. Bad evals are worse than no evals — they give false confidence. Eval datasets go stale as the product evolves and need active maintenance.

name: Spec-Driven AI Development
description: The OpenAPI spec is the source of truth. Define it once in Swagger — Speakeasy generates typed SDKs automatically, and every AI coding session in Cursor and Claude Code is grounded in that contract.
target: Teams building AI-native APIs and products where spec drift is a real problem
mission: Your API has 40 endpoints. The docs are wrong, the SDKs are hand-written and out of date, and the AI coding assistant is improvising because there is no structured contract to work from.

not_in_stack.reason: UI generation tool — irrelevant when the product is an API, not a frontend
Visual workflow builder; spec-driven work requires code-level precision and version control
Observability layer — this stack focuses on the development and generation phase, not runtime
Claude Code already handles spec-guided coding when grounded in your OpenAPI file — adding a separate AI spec-writing tool duplicates effort at the authoring layer.

kill_conditions: Spec discipline breaks down under deadline pressure and drift reappears
API surface stabilises and spec maintenance overhead exceeds its value
Team moves to a monolith where SDKs and multi-language clients are no longer needed
why: Spec Kit creates structured context that steers Claude Code and Cursor toward well-defined outcomes instead of improvised prompts. Swagger defines the API contract and feeds its context directly into Cursor; Speakeasy auto-generates SDKs when the spec changes; Langfuse keeps AI calls observable throughout. Everything downstream of the spec stays in sync.
tradeoffs: Upfront spec investment slows initial velocity — not worth it for throwaway prototypes or highly exploratory projects. The discipline to keep specs current requires team buy-in; a stale spec is worse than no spec.

name: LLM Cost Reduction Stack
description: Already in production. Cut LLM spend by 50–80% without degrading user-facing quality.
target: Teams whose LLM API bill has become a line item that leadership is questioning
mission: Your LLM bill hit $15k last month. Leadership wants it halved by next quarter. You can't touch the product. You need to optimize the infrastructure underneath it.

not_in_stack.reason: Frontend SDK — cost optimization happens at the routing and model-selection layer, not the UI
Adding orchestration complexity during a cost-reduction sprint introduces new failure modes
IDE assistant; this is an infra change, not a feature build

kill_conditions: Cost is driven by retrieval volume, not generation — switch focus to vector DB and chunking optimization
Quality degradation from cheaper models becomes a retention problem
Traffic patterns become unpredictable enough to require dedicated capacity planning
why: LiteLLM is the routing layer — it scores each request by complexity and routes cheap tasks to smaller, cheaper models while reserving frontier models for tasks that need them. OpenRouter provides access to the cheapest available model for each capability tier. Ollama handles dev and test traffic at zero cost, eliminating API calls during development. Promptfoo validates that cheaper model substitutes maintain acceptable quality before the route goes live. Langfuse shows per-request cost breakdowns and flags which endpoints are burning budget.
tradeoffs: Model routing by complexity requires prompt classifiers or heuristics that add latency and engineering work. Quality regressions from cheaper models are subtle and hard to catch without comprehensive evals already in place. Cost reduction often surfaces latent quality issues that were previously masked by expensive model performance.

name: Multi-Modal RAG Stack
description: RAG over PDFs, images, tables, and charts — not just text chunks. Retrieval that understands documents the way humans do.
target: Teams building document Q&A systems where the documents contain charts, diagrams, tables, and mixed-format content
mission: Your RAG system handles text well but falls apart on PDFs with tables, financial reports with charts, and slide decks with diagrams. Users keep asking questions the system can't answer.

not_in_stack.reason: LlamaIndex's multimodal parsing is significantly more mature for mixed-format documents
In-process DB lacks the metadata filtering and multi-vector support complex multimodal retrieval requires
Storing image embeddings alongside text embeddings needs dedicated vector infrastructure

kill_conditions: Document types expand to video or audio, requiring a different ingestion pipeline entirely
Retrieval quality plateaus and fine-tuning the embedding model becomes the next lever
Document volume reaches a scale that requires distributed indexing infrastructure
why: LlamaIndex's multimodal capabilities parse PDFs, extract tables, and understand image context natively. OpenAI API provides both vision models for image understanding and text-embedding-3 for dense retrieval. Qdrant handles multi-vector storage — text embeddings and image embeddings live in the same collection with payload filtering. LangGraph manages the retrieval routing logic — deciding when to use vision, when to use text search, and when to combine both. Langfuse traces retrieval quality so you can identify which document types are degrading accuracy.
tradeoffs: Multimodal retrieval is significantly more expensive than text-only — vision model calls add cost and latency. Table extraction accuracy degrades on complex merged-cell layouts. Retrieval quality evaluation requires human annotation of ground truth for non-text content.

name: Legacy App + AI Stack
description: Add AI capabilities to an existing application without a rewrite. Language-agnostic, side-car friendly, incrementally deployable.
target: Engineering teams with a working Rails, Django, Java, or .NET app who want to add AI features without blowing up the existing stack
mission: You have a 6-year-old Django monolith. Product wants a document summarization feature by end of quarter. Rewriting the backend in TypeScript is not an option anyone is discussing.

not_in_stack.reason: TypeScript/Next.js SDK — useless if your app is in Python, Ruby, or Java
TypeScript-native framework — assumes you're building in TypeScript from the start
Full agent framework adds complexity before you've validated basic AI integration works

kill_conditions: AI features grow complex enough to justify a dedicated AI service boundary with its own stack
Legacy app gets rebuilt and the constraint of language-agnosticism disappears
Volume requires a dedicated inference infrastructure layer
why: LiteLLM acts as an AI sidecar — it exposes an OpenAI-compatible REST endpoint that any existing service can call with no stack changes. Your Java service, Ruby app, or PHP monolith makes a single HTTP call and gets model responses back, completely unaware of what's behind it. LangChain sits between that endpoint and application logic when you need Python-side orchestration. Langfuse captures traces from the sidecar layer passively — your legacy application code never knows it's there. The critical property is provider portability: because everything routes through LiteLLM, you can swap from OpenAI to Anthropic or a self-hosted model with a one-line config change, no code touches required.
tradeoffs: Network hop to a sidecar LLM proxy adds latency. Without shared context between the legacy app and the AI layer, complex stateful AI features require careful API design. This is an integration pattern, not an architecture — technical debt will accumulate if AI features grow significantly.

name: Enterprise RAG Stack
description: Mature, well-observed, enterprise-proven. Full RAG pipeline with routing, eval, and observability from day one.
target: Internal knowledge bases, customer support bots, and document Q&A at scale
mission: Your company has 200k internal documents. Support agents spend half their day finding answers that are definitely written down somewhere. That institutional knowledge needs to be instantly queryable.

not_in_stack.reason: Visual builder lacks the programmatic control needed for production RAG eval and routing logic
In-process DB not suitable for multi-tenant production workloads with SLA requirements
Frontend streaming SDK — enterprise RAG serves multiple internal clients, not one web app

kill_conditions: Document corpus grows to billions of vectors requiring a managed cloud vector service
Retrieval quality ceiling is traced to the base model and fine-tuning becomes the lever
Compliance team requires data access audit logs that require a different observability stack
why: Enterprise RAG needs more than a simple chain — it needs routing, branching retrieval logic, and evaluation you can run in CI. LangGraph handles complex multi-step retrieval; pgvector keeps sensitive data in your existing Postgres; LiteLLM gives you model flexibility without lock-in, routing to both OpenAI and Anthropic; LangSmith traces every step so you can debug retrieval quality and catch regressions.
tradeoffs: High complexity and ops overhead. Overkill for simple Q&A or small-scale use. Requires a team with LLM engineering experience to instrument evals properly and maintain retrieval quality as data grows.

name: OSS Self-Hosted AI Stack
description: Zero data egress; entirely on-premise. Full AI capabilities without sending a single byte to an external service.
target: Privacy-first orgs, regulated industries, and teams with strict data residency requirements
mission: Your legal team just informed you that sending any customer data to a third-party API requires a DPA you don't have. You need full AI capability and full data control.

not_in_stack.reason: Every API call sends data to OpenAI's servers — that's the exact constraint you're solving
Cloud-hosted Langfuse sends trace data externally — self-host it or replace with logfire
Managed cloud vector DB — data residency requires a self-hosted alternative

kill_conditions: Team grows to a scale where GPU ops cost exceeds managed cloud inference
Model capability requirements exceed what open weights can provide for your use case
Compliance framework gets updated to permit specific EU-hosted cloud providers
why: Ollama and vLLM handle two different traffic patterns that co-exist in the same stack. Ollama serves interactive traffic — developer queries, ad-hoc analysis, low-concurrency requests — with a friendly local API. vLLM handles production throughput — batched requests, parallel generations, the load that would make Ollama fall over. LlamaIndex builds the retrieval layer that feeds both, embedding documents and routing queries. Qdrant stores vectors on your own infrastructure with no external call. Langfuse traces on the same private network. The architecture enforces a data boundary: no content, no queries, and no traces cross your perimeter. Useful when your threat model includes data residency — or when you want full control over the cost curve.
tradeoffs: You own the entire ops burden. Smaller open models have a lower capability ceiling than GPT-4o or Claude — expect more prompt engineering work to get comparable output quality. Infra and GPU costs can exceed managed services at scale.

name: EU / GDPR Regulated AI Stack
description: Cloud-hosted AI that legally works in the EU — using providers with EU data residency, DPAs, and GDPR-compliant infrastructure.
target: EU-based companies and teams handling EU citizen data who need cloud AI without self-hosting the entire stack
mission: You're building in Germany. Your DPO has approved cloud services only from EU-resident providers with signed DPAs. OpenAI is not on the approved list.

not_in_stack.reason: Data processed on US servers by default; no EU data residency option currently available
US-based provider without EU data residency offering at this time
US-based managed service without EU data residency guarantees

kill_conditions: Compliance requirements become strict enough to require full on-premises processing
EU AI Act obligations demand model cards and transparency documentation the provider can't supply
Required capabilities don't exist in EU-resident providers
why: The stack is built around one hard constraint: no data crosses jurisdictional boundaries. Mistral AI processes in France under EU law — the LLM call is the only place content leaves your environment, and it stays in-region. Qdrant self-hosted in an EU data center means retrieval over sensitive documents never touches a US cloud region. Langfuse self-hosted keeps trace data on the same network as your application. LiteLLM acts as a routing layer that can hard-block non-EU provider endpoints — compliance is enforced at the infrastructure level, not by developer discipline. Logfire provides Python-native observability without routing telemetry to US-based SaaS. The architecture is less about any individual tool and more about enforcing a data boundary that holds under audit.
tradeoffs: EU-resident AI providers have smaller model selection and lower benchmark scores than US frontier models. Self-hosting components in EU infrastructure means you inherit the ops burden. The approved provider list changes as regulations and provider offerings evolve.

name: Edge / On-Device AI Stack
description: AI that runs entirely on the device — no server round-trip, no API bill, no data leaving the machine. Ollama serves the model, LlamaIndex builds the RAG pipeline, Chroma stores the local vector index.
target: Teams building offline-capable apps, privacy-sensitive tooling, or low-latency AI that can't afford a network hop
mission: Your app needs to work on an airplane. Or in a hospital where nothing talks to the internet. Or for users who refuse to send their data to any server. The AI runs on the device or it doesn't run.

not_in_stack.reason: Cloud API — requires internet connectivity and sends data to external servers
Cloud observability — edge deployments trace locally or not at all
High-throughput server inference framework — requires GPU server infrastructure
Ollama already uses llama.cpp under the hood — listing both creates redundancy without adding value.

kill_conditions: Feature complexity requires models larger than the device can run
Multi-user coordination requires shared server state that per-device inference can't provide
Regulatory requirements mandate auditability that client-side inference can't satisfy
why: llama.cpp is the portability layer — it runs 4-bit quantized models on CPU with no GPU required, which means the same binary works on a MacBook, a Raspberry Pi, or an embedded Linux device. Ollama wraps llama.cpp with a local REST API so application code makes the same HTTP call it would make to OpenAI — the deployment target is invisible to the app layer. LlamaIndex builds a retrieval pipeline on top of the local model, enabling document Q&A without any external requests. The architectural consequence: take this stack offline completely. No API keys, no network round-trip, no data leaving the device. The performance constraint is quantization — you trade some model quality for the ability to run anywhere.
tradeoffs: Model size is severely constrained by device memory — you're working with 3B–13B parameter models, not 70B+. Output quality is noticeably lower for complex reasoning. Battery and thermal impact on mobile devices is significant. Models need to be bundled with or downloaded to the device.

name: AI Red-Team / Security Stack
description: Test your own AI systems for prompt injection, jailbreaks, and harmful outputs before attackers do. Promptfoo runs adversarial inputs; DeepEval scores safety and toxicity metrics; Langfuse captures every result for review.
target: Security engineers and ML teams hardening AI products before launch or after an incident
mission: Your AI product ships next month. You don't know if it can be jailbroken, manipulated into leaking system prompts, or coaxed into generating harmful content. You need to find out before users do.

not_in_stack.reason: Orchestration framework — not designed for adversarial prompt testing
IDE assistant; red-teaming is a testing discipline, not a development one
Production observability tool — the adversarial test harness handles its own trace capture
RAGAS is scoped to RAG retrieval quality — not applicable to a general adversarial red-team setup.

kill_conditions: Security findings require architectural changes that a red-team alone can't drive
Regulatory framework mandates a formal third-party security audit that replaces internal red-teaming
Attack surface stabilises post-launch and ongoing red-teaming shifts to a lighter monitoring posture
why: Promptfoo's red-team mode runs adversarial prompt suites — injection attempts, jailbreak sequences, PII extraction attempts — against your system and reports pass/fail with evidence. DeepEval adds structured safety metrics: toxicity scores, bias detection, and hallucination rates across test datasets. RAGAS provides RAG-specific security evals — testing whether retrieval can be manipulated to surface unauthorized documents. OpenAI API acts as both the target system and the judge model for automated scoring.
tradeoffs: Automated red-teaming catches known attack patterns but not novel adversarial techniques. False negatives are more dangerous than false positives here — a passing test suite is not a security guarantee. Human red-teamers with domain expertise remain essential alongside automated tooling.

name: Fine-Tuning Pipeline
description: When prompting hits its ceiling, you train. Curate data, fine-tune a base model, evaluate before and after, and serve the result.
target: Teams who've exhausted prompt engineering and need a model that truly understands their domain, format, or persona
mission: You've spent 6 months prompt-engineering a medical coding assistant. It's still wrong 15% of the time on edge cases that any trained coder would catch. The base model doesn't know your domain. You need to teach it.

not_in_stack.reason: Inference orchestration layer — irrelevant during the training phase
Replaced by Weights & Biases here — W&B is the standard for training experiment tracking
IDE assistant; fine-tuning pipelines run in notebooks and training scripts, not editor workflows

kill_conditions: Training data quality and diversity plateau before the model reaches target accuracy
Compute cost of fine-tuning exceeds the cost of better prompting or RAG augmentation
Regulatory environment restricts training on customer data
why: HuggingFace connects the model zoo to your training loop — you pull a base model and TRL executes the fine-tuning run. TRL is the actual training engine: it implements SFT, DPO, GRPO, and RLHF trainers with native Weights & Biases integration so every run streams metrics and sample outputs automatically. W&B lets you compare runs and spot overfitting before you waste compute. DeepEval evaluates the fine-tuned checkpoint against the base model on structured quality benchmarks before it's promoted — the eval gate is what separates a fine-tune from a regression. vLLM serves the promoted checkpoint with optimized batching; Replicate handles deployment when you don't control GPU infrastructure. The flow is linear and gated: no checkpoint advances without passing DeepEval, and W&B gives you the data to explain why.
tradeoffs: Fine-tuning requires high-quality labeled data — typically 1k–100k examples depending on task complexity. Training costs scale with model size and dataset volume. Fine-tuned models can catastrophically forget general capabilities while gaining domain-specific ones. Maintaining fine-tuned models through base model updates requires ongoing retraining.

name: Document Intelligence Stack
description: Structured extraction from unstructured documents at volume. Not Q&A — extraction. Contracts, invoices, medical records, regulatory filings.
target: Teams processing high volumes of structured business documents who need reliable field extraction, not conversational retrieval
mission: You receive 2,000 supplier contracts a month. Legal needs 14 specific fields extracted from each one. Humans are doing it. It costs $40 per contract. You need 95% accuracy at $0.10 per contract.

not_in_stack.reason: General orchestration; LlamaIndex's document parsing is purpose-built for this extraction pattern
In-process vector DB — high-volume extraction pipelines need persistent, queryable storage
Multi-step agent orchestration; extraction is a structured, repeatable transform, not an agent task

kill_conditions: Extraction accuracy requirements exceed what LLM-based extraction can achieve without fine-tuning
Document format diversity grows to include video, audio, or non-text media
Volume scales to a point requiring dedicated document processing infrastructure
why: LlamaIndex's document parsers handle PDFs, Word docs, and Excel files with table and layout awareness. OpenAI API's structured output mode enforces extraction into typed schemas — fields either populate correctly or the failure is explicit. Qdrant stores extracted embeddings with rich metadata so documents are queryable by extracted field values, not just semantic similarity. Langfuse traces extraction quality per document type so you can identify which formats need prompt tuning.
tradeoffs: LLM extraction accuracy varies significantly with document layout complexity. Headers, footers, and merged cells in tables are common failure modes. Structured output mode reduces hallucination but not layout misreading. Ground-truth validation datasets are essential and expensive to build.

name: Research & Synthesis Stack
description: AI that reads across sources, connects evidence, and produces structured reports — not a chatbot, a research analyst.
target: Research teams, analysts, and knowledge workers who need to synthesize large bodies of source material into structured outputs
mission: Your analyst team spends 3 days per week reading papers, news, and reports to write a single 10-page synthesis document. The reading is tractable. The connecting-the-dots is where the value is.

not_in_stack.reason: Visual workflow builder — research synthesis requires programmatic multi-source retrieval strategies
Frontend streaming SDK — synthesis pipelines run as background jobs, not interactive web requests
Corpus-scale semantic search needs a dedicated vector DB, not an extension

kill_conditions: Source material is too dynamic to index — real-time synthesis requires live search integration
Output quality requires domain-specific fine-tuning beyond what a general model provides
Corpus grows to a scale requiring distributed indexing beyond a single vector DB instance
why: The hard problem in research synthesis isn't retrieval — it's merging evidence from sources with different scope and provenance without hallucinating connections. LangChain queries each corpus in parallel — research literature, internal documents, web sources — then merges the retrieved context before generation, giving the model a richer window than any single source provides. Qdrant stores each corpus with metadata so searches can filter by source, date, and domain before merging. OpenAI API handles both dense embedding and the synthesis generation. Promptfoo evaluates synthesis quality against reference outputs before results reach a stakeholder — automated quality gates matter when the output is a high-stakes deliverable. Langfuse traces every retrieval decision so analysts can audit exactly which source passages informed each claim in the output.
tradeoffs: Synthesis quality is highly sensitive to retrieval quality — the model can only connect dots it can find. Citation accuracy and faithfulness to sources degrade as corpus size increases without retrieval quality work. Building evaluation datasets for synthesis tasks requires expensive expert annotation.

name: Memory-Augmented Agent Stack
description: Agents that remember users, decisions, and session history across conversations — not by stuffing the context window, but by storing and retrieving structured memory.
target: Teams building AI assistants, support bots, and agents that serve returning users over multiple sessions
mission: Your agent handles 20-turn support conversations. On session 5, the user has to re-explain their entire situation from scratch. The agent has no memory of what was decided two weeks ago. That stops here.

not_in_stack.reason: LangGraph provides tighter per-step state control for multi-turn agents that need memory at each decision point
Frontend streaming SDK — memory augmentation happens at the agent orchestration layer, not the UI layer
RAG over documents; Mem0 handles episodic memory — what users said and decided, not what documents contain

kill_conditions: Memory growth causes retrieval latency to exceed user-experience thresholds
User data privacy requirements prevent storing conversation history in third-party memory systems
Context window growth makes full in-context conversation history sufficient for your use case
why: LangGraph manages the agent state machine — it decides when to read memory before responding and when to write memory after a turn. Mem0 compresses episodic memory into a retrieval-ready format: it extracts facts, preferences, and decisions from past conversations and surfaces only the relevant ones, reducing context tokens by up to 80% while keeping the agent contextually aware. Qdrant stores the underlying memory embeddings with rich metadata for filtering by user, session, and time. OpenAI API handles the reasoning turn with the memory-enriched context. Langfuse traces every memory read and write so you can audit exactly what the agent remembered and when. The pattern unlocks a fundamentally different product: an agent that treats every user as a returning customer, not a stranger.
tradeoffs: Memory quality depends on extraction quality — poorly structured past conversations produce unreliable facts. Memory staleness is a real problem: what was true three months ago may not be true today, and stale memory can actively mislead the agent. Memory retrieval adds latency (~50–150ms per turn). Privacy implications of persistent user memory require a GDPR-compliant storage strategy from day one.

name: AI Guardrails Stack
description: Runtime enforcement layer that prevents PII leakage, blocks jailbreaks, enforces topic scope, and validates structured outputs — on every LLM response, in production.
target: Teams deploying LLM features to production who need safety guarantees that go beyond prompt engineering
mission: Your LLM product shipped. Last week it responded to 'ignore all instructions' with a partial system prompt. The week before it included a user's email in a response to a different user. You need enforcement at the infrastructure layer — not a better system prompt.

not_in_stack.reason: Pre-launch adversarial testing — use it before deploying. Guardrails are runtime enforcement after deployment. You need both.
Orchestration framework; guardrails sit below the application code at the infrastructure layer
Agent orchestration; guardrails apply universally to all LLM calls regardless of agent structure

kill_conditions: Guardrail latency overhead exceeds product tolerance for response time
Compliance framework requires certified AI safety tooling with formal audit trails that OSS tools don't yet provide
Output validation complexity grows to require a full ML safety team rather than a rules-based system
why: LiteLLM acts as the routing and enforcement gateway — all LLM calls pass through it before reaching OpenAI or any other provider. NeMo Guardrails intercepts at the conversation level: it enforces topic limits ('this agent only discusses billing'), detects jailbreak patterns, and blocks responses that violate defined dialog policies using the Colang DSL. Guardrails AI validates at the output level: it checks that responses conform to a schema, don't contain PII, and pass custom validators you define in Python. Langfuse captures every enforcement event so you can audit which guardrails fired, when, and why. The architecture creates defense-in-depth: NeMo stops the model from reasoning in forbidden directions; Guardrails AI validates the output even if it does.
tradeoffs: Every guardrail adds latency — expect 50–200ms overhead per request depending on rule complexity. Overly aggressive topic rails frustrate users with false positives. NeMo Guardrails' Colang DSL has a learning curve. Guardrails AI validators require maintenance as output schemas evolve. Neither tool prevents a sufficiently sophisticated adversarial attack — guardrails reduce risk, they don't eliminate it.

name: Graph RAG Stack
description: Retrieval that understands entity relationships, not just semantic similarity. Answers multi-hop questions that flat vector search cannot handle.
target: Teams with corpora where relationships between entities matter — legal documents, research literature, technical knowledge bases
mission: Your RAG system answers 'what does clause 12 say?' but can't answer 'which clauses across these three contracts conflict with each other?' You need retrieval that reasons over relationships, not just text similarity.

not_in_stack.reason: LightRAG handles its own document ingestion and graph construction — adding LlamaIndex creates a redundant ingestion layer
General orchestration layer; LightRAG's dual-level retrieval is self-contained and doesn't require a separate chain orchestrator
Managed vector DB without the graph storage and traversal capabilities LightRAG's global retrieval mode requires

kill_conditions: Knowledge graph grows to a scale requiring a dedicated graph database like Neo4j
Source document quality is too noisy for reliable entity extraction — requires data cleaning before indexing
Multi-hop reasoning complexity exceeds what LightRAG's entity extraction can model accurately
why: LightRAG builds a knowledge graph from your documents during ingestion — it extracts entities and relationships using an LLM pass, then indexes them in a graph structure alongside the standard vector index. At query time it uses dual-level retrieval: local retrieval for specific facts (like standard dense search) and global retrieval for thematic or relational questions that require traversing entity relationships. The result is retrieval that can answer 'which provisions in these contracts are in tension?' not just 'what does this clause say?' Qdrant stores the vector component with metadata for filtering. Langfuse traces every retrieval decision so you can audit which graph paths were traversed. EMNLP 2025 benchmarks show LightRAG achieves ~30% lower latency than standard RAG on complex multi-hop queries while delivering meaningfully better answers on relationship-dependent questions.
tradeoffs: Graph construction requires an LLM pass over every document at ingestion time — significantly more expensive than chunk-embed indexing. Entity extraction quality degrades on noisy, poorly formatted documents. The global retrieval mode over large graphs can be slow. Debugging why a retrieval failed requires understanding both the vector index and the graph structure. Not worth the overhead for corpora where every question is self-contained in a single passage.

name: Vibe Coding Stack
description: From natural-language description to deployed full-stack app in hours. No Figma file, no component library, no prior coding experience required.
target: Non-engineer founders, PMs, and designers who need a working full-stack prototype without an engineering bottleneck
mission: You have a product idea and 48 hours. You're not a developer. Lovable turns your description into a working full-stack app with auth, database, and UI. Cursor and Claude Code extend it when you need something custom.

not_in_stack.reason: UI component generator — Lovable generates the entire full-stack application including backend logic and database schema, not just components
Complex agent orchestration is premature at the prototype stage — validate the idea first, then add infrastructure
Python-first framework; Lovable's generated stack is TypeScript/React — adding Python introduces a language boundary before the product is proven

kill_conditions: Generated code accumulates enough technical debt that a ground-up rebuild is faster than iterating on AI-generated output
Product requires complex backend logic that generation tools can't reliably produce
Team grows to include engineers who need proper engineering standards and codebase ownership
why: Lovable generates production-grade React/TypeScript full-stack applications from natural-language prompts — not wireframes, not mockups, but running apps with auth, database connections, and API integrations. The starting point is already deployable, which is the critical difference from tools like v0. Cursor takes ownership when you need changes that require code reasoning — editing generated logic, debugging API integrations, adding custom business rules. Claude Code handles cross-file ripple effects: when you change a data model, it updates every component, route, and test that references it. The Vercel AI SDK connects the app to any LLM capability you want to add. Langfuse keeps the AI calls observable so you can see costs and failures before they surprise you. The stack is deliberately beginner-friendly: at every step, the AI output is already a working artefact that can be deployed and tested.
tradeoffs: Lovable-generated code is production-grade in structure but not in engineering discipline — it lacks tests, has inconsistent error handling, and accrues technical debt quickly without active maintenance. Complex or domain-specific business logic often requires manual correction. Debugging AI-generated code you didn't write yourself has a steep learning curve for non-engineers. The stack is optimized for speed-to-deployed-demo, not maintainability.

name: Async AI Coding Team
description: A hybrid coding workflow where Jules handles the async ticket queue overnight, Claude Code manages interactive sessions, and Windsurf keeps every engineer in flow state throughout the day.
target: Engineering teams with well-specified backlogs who want AI completing the deterministic half of their tickets
mission: You have 4 engineers and 80 backlog tickets. Half are well-specified and repetitive. You want AI completing those overnight so engineers wake up to closed tickets, not a blank editor and a full queue.

not_in_stack.reason: Windsurf replaces Cursor here as the team's primary editor — use one AI editor consistently, not two simultaneously
Overlaps with Jules for the async execution role — Jules integrates more natively with GitHub's async PR workflow and runs in isolated VMs
Explicit state machine orchestration adds complexity — Jules and Claude Code manage their own execution state natively without a coordinator

kill_conditions: Task specifications are too ambiguous for autonomous execution without constant human correction
Security policy prohibits agents with write access to production repositories
Agent failure rate on complex tasks exceeds the cost of equivalent human engineering time
why: The stack creates two parallel execution modes that complement each other. Jules handles the async queue: assign it well-specified tickets at end of day, it runs in isolated VMs, and PRs appear in GitHub by morning — no human keyboard input required. Claude Code handles interactive mode: complex tasks that need reasoning mid-run, architectural decisions, and anything Jules gets stuck on. Windsurf keeps every engineer in flow state with IDE-level completions that have full codebase context. GitHub MCP connects both agents to live repo state. CodeRabbit reviews every PR — from Jules or Claude Code — before a human sees it. LangSmith traces every agent run so debugging failures is straightforward. The leverage: engineers spend their time on the hard 50% of tickets, not the repetitive 50%.
tradeoffs: Jules requires well-specified tickets to succeed — vague acceptance criteria produce vague PRs that need significant rework. CodeRabbit review creates a bottleneck at high PR volume if humans don't stay current on the queue. Running Jules + Claude Code + Windsurf simultaneously on a large repo creates significant monthly token costs that scale with team size. Agent failures mid-run need human intervention and can block async queue throughput.

name: Real-Time Voice Agent Stack
description: Sub-500ms voice pipeline with code-level control: Pipecat orchestrates the STT→LLM→TTS loop, Deepgram transcribes in real time, LiveKit handles WebRTC, and ElevenLabs speaks.
target: Teams who've outgrown Vapi's abstractions and need code-level control over VAD, latency, and custom speech models
mission: The managed platform works but you've hit its ceiling. You need custom interruption handling, sub-400ms end-to-end latency, a fine-tuned ASR model for your medical vocabulary, and the ability to run the entire pipeline on your own infrastructure. That requires owning the code.

not_in_stack.reason: Managed platform — the right starting point, but its abstractions become a ceiling when you need VAD tuning, custom ASR, or WebRTC control. Graduate to this stack when you hit it.
Frontend streaming SDK; Pipecat's frame-based pipeline handles audio streaming architecture natively with sub-frame latency control
General model hosting can't match the sub-300ms streaming latency that purpose-built Deepgram and ElevenLabs APIs deliver

kill_conditions: Domain-specific vocabulary degrades Deepgram accuracy below acceptable threshold and a fine-tuned ASR is needed
Call volume grows to the point where a managed platform becomes cost-effective again
Real-time video + voice features require full daily.co or Agora integration beyond LiveKit's scope
why: Pipecat's frame-based pipeline is the breakthrough: it processes audio as a stream of frames rather than as discrete request/response cycles, which is what enables sub-500ms end-to-end latency. Each frame flows through Deepgram (STT at ~300ms), through the LLM turn (OpenAI API), and out through ElevenLabs (TTS) as a continuous stream — not a waterfall. LiveKit provides the WebRTC infrastructure that routes that audio stream reliably at scale, with built-in multi-participant support and edge node distribution. Langfuse traces every call turn with timing data so you can identify which stage — STT, LLM, or TTS — is causing latency spikes. The result is a voice agent pipeline you own completely: custom VAD models, provider swapping, on-premise deployment, and latency tuning at every stage.
tradeoffs: Owning the pipeline means owning the ops. WebRTC infrastructure, Pipecat frame handling, and multi-provider integration all require engineering investment. Debugging latency regressions across STT/LLM/TTS boundaries is non-trivial without strong telemetry. The capability ceiling is much higher than Vapi, but so is the complexity floor.
